purpose.description: The Acme Design System provides a shared library of reusable components, design tokens, and patterns that enable product teams to build consistent, accessible user interfaces across web, iOS, and Android platforms. It encodes Acme's design language into a single source of truth so that designers and engineers work from the same specifications.

useCases.description: When building a new product or feature within the Acme product suite that must conform to the Acme brand and interaction standards.
When an existing product is being redesigned or migrated to a modern front-end stack and needs a consistent component library.
When contributing a new component or pattern that will be shared across multiple Acme products.
When building a white-label product that must not carry the Acme brand identity.

alternative.name: custom theme or independent design system
alternative.rationale: The Acme Design System encodes Acme-specific brand decisions (color palette, typography, iconography). White-label products require a neutral foundation that can be re-skinned per customer. Use the token architecture as inspiration, but do not depend on Acme-branded tokens directly.

useCases.description: When building a one-off marketing landing page with highly custom, editorial layouts that intentionally break grid and component conventions.

alternative.name: bespoke implementation with brand guidelines
alternative.rationale: Marketing pages often require creative freedom that a component system constrains. Use the brand color tokens and typography scale for consistency, but do not force editorial layouts into rigid component patterns.

bestPractices.guidance: Use semantic tokens instead of raw color, spacing, or typography values in all production code.
bestPractices.rationale: Semantic tokens abstract the intent (e.g., 'color-text-primary') from the value (e.g., '#1a1a1a'). This enables theming, dark mode, and system-wide visual changes from a single source of truth without touching component code.

bestPractices.guidance: Do not modify or override design token values at the component level. Use the token as-is or request a new token.
bestPractices.rationale: Overriding tokens breaks the contract between the design system and consuming products. When the system updates a token value, overridden components will not pick up the change, creating visual drift.

bestPractices.guidance: Test all components at 200% browser zoom to ensure content does not overflow, overlap, or become inaccessible.
bestPractices.rationale: WCAG 1.4.4 requires that content remains functional at 200% text size. Components that break at zoom levels below 200% fail this criterion and exclude users with low vision.

bestPractices.guidance: Prefer composition over customization. Combine existing components to build new patterns rather than adding configuration props to existing components.
bestPractices.rationale: Every prop added to a component increases its API surface, testing burden, and cognitive load. Composing simple components into larger patterns keeps each component focused and predictable.

bestPractices.guidance: Every interactive component must be fully operable with a keyboard alone — no mouse or touch required.
bestPractices.rationale: Keyboard operability is the foundation of accessibility. Users who rely on switch devices, screen readers, or alternative input methods all depend on keyboard support as the lowest common denominator.

bestPractices.guidance: Pin the design system package version in production applications. Upgrade deliberately, not automatically.
bestPractices.rationale: Automatic upgrades can introduce visual regressions or breaking API changes. Pinning versions ensures stability while allowing teams to upgrade on their own schedule after testing.

bestPractices.guidance: Do not wrap design system components in additional DOM elements that alter their semantics or accessibility tree.
bestPractices.rationale: Wrapping a button in a div with an onClick handler, or nesting a link inside a button, creates unexpected behavior for assistive technology. Use components as designed — their semantic structure is intentional.

bestPractices.guidance: When contributing a new component, provide at minimum: a purpose guideline, an API guideline, and a best-practices guideline before publishing to production.
bestPractices.rationale: Components without documentation are components without a contract. Consumers cannot use them correctly if the intended use cases, API, and constraints are not documented.

documentation.name: Acme Design System — Starter Kit
documentation.description: A representative sample of components, tokens, a style, and a pattern demonstrating DSDS document structure.

metadata.value: A clickable element that triggers an action. Buttons are the primary mechanism for submitting forms, opening dialogs, confirming decisions, and initiating operations.

items.name: emphasis
items.description: The visual weight of the button, indicating the relative importance of the action.

values.name: primary
values.description: The highest-emphasis button. Used for the single most important action on the surface.

values.name: secondary
values.description: A medium-emphasis button with a visible border. Used alongside a primary button for secondary actions.

values.name: ghost
values.description: A low-emphasis button with no border or background. Used for tertiary actions in dense layouts.

items.name: size
items.description: Controls padding, font size, and minimum touch target area.

values.name: small
values.description: Compact size for dense layouts such as toolbars and table rows.

values.name: large
values.description: Large size for hero surfaces and mobile-first layouts.

items.name: default
items.description: The resting state of the button.

items.name: hover
items.description: Visual feedback when the pointer is over the button.

items.name: focus
items.description: Visible focus ring when the button receives keyboard focus.

items.name: disabled
items.description: Non-interactive state. The button is visible but cannot be activated.

useCases.description: When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form, saving data, or opening a dialog.
When a destructive action needs to be initiated, such as deleting a record. Pair with a confirmation dialog.
When the action navigates to a different page or URL.

alternative.name: link
alternative.rationale: Links carry native navigation semantics. Screen readers announce them as links, and browsers support open-in-new-tab.

items.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary button.
items.rationale: Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy.

items.guidance: Use the loading state instead of disabling the button during async operations.
items.rationale: A disabled button gives no feedback that an action is in progress.

items.guidance: When using an icon-only button, provide an aria-label.
items.rationale: Screen readers need a text equivalent for every interactive control.

agents.intent: Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.

constraints.rule: Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.
Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.
Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.

disambiguation.distinction: Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.
Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.

antiPatterns.description: Using a button to navigate to another page.
antiPatterns.instead: Use a link element with href.

antiPatterns.description: Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.
antiPatterns.instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.

metadata.value: A navigational element that takes the user to a different page or URL.

useCases.description: When the action navigates the user to a different page, section, or external resource.
When the action triggers a state change or submits data without navigation.

alternative.name: button
alternative.rationale: Buttons have action semantics. Using a link for non-navigation actions creates a mismatch between role and behavior.

items.guidance: Always pair link color with an underline for inline links within body text.
items.rationale: Color alone is insufficient to distinguish links for users with color vision deficiency.

agents.intent: Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.

constraints.rule: Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.
Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.
Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.

disambiguation.distinction: Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.
Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.

antiPatterns.description: Using a button to navigate to another page.
antiPatterns.instead: Use a link element with href.

antiPatterns.description: Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.
antiPatterns.instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.

metadata.value: A message bar that communicates status, errors, warnings, or informational messages to the user.

items.name: severity
items.description: The visual treatment and semantic meaning of the alert.

values.name: warning
values.description: Warns about a potential issue that doesn't block the user.

values.name: error
values.description: Communicates an error that requires attention.

items.guidance: Use role='alert' or aria-live='assertive' for error alerts so screen readers announce them immediately.
items.rationale: Without a live region, dynamically injected error messages are invisible to assistive technology.

agents.intent: Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.

constraints.rule: Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.
Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.
Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.

disambiguation.distinction: Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.
Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.

antiPatterns.description: Using a button to navigate to another page.
antiPatterns.instead: Use a link element with href.

antiPatterns.description: Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.
antiPatterns.instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.

metadata.value: The primary interactive color. Used for primary buttons and key interactive elements.

agents.intent: Apply the primary text color for body content, headings, and labels on default background surfaces.

constraints.rule: Do not use on dark or colored background surfaces.
Do not override this token value at the component level.

disambiguation.distinction: Use primary for default reading content; use secondary for supporting or reduced-emphasis text.
Use primary on default surfaces; use on-action inside filled interactive components.

metadata.value: The default text color. Used for body text, headings, and labels on standard backgrounds.

agents.intent: Apply the primary text color for body content, headings, and labels on default background surfaces.

constraints.rule: Do not use on dark or colored background surfaces.
Do not override this token value at the component level.

disambiguation.distinction: Use primary for default reading content; use secondary for supporting or reduced-emphasis text.
Use primary on default surfaces; use on-action inside filled interactive components.

metadata.value: Base spacing unit. The most commonly used spacing value across components and layouts.

agents.intent: Apply the primary text color for body content, headings, and labels on default background surfaces.

constraints.rule: Do not use on dark or colored background surfaces.
Do not override this token value at the component level.

disambiguation.distinction: Use primary for default reading content; use secondary for supporting or reduced-emphasis text.
Use primary on default surfaces; use on-action inside filled interactive components.

agents.intent: Define and organize a set of related design tokens, providing shared defaults for type and category.

constraints.rule: Do not reference base palette tokens directly in component code.
constraints.context: Always reference semantic tokens that point to palette values.

metadata.value: A spatial system built on a 4px base unit for consistent whitespace across all surfaces.

items.name: spacing
items.role: Provides all spatial tokens from space-0 through space-8.

items.title: Use the scale
items.description: Every spacing value must reference a token from the spacing scale. No hard-coded pixel values.

items.title: Density over decoration
items.description: Spacing exists to create clear relationships between elements, not to fill empty areas.

documentBlocks.name: spacing-scale
documentBlocks.description: A geometric scale based on a 4px unit.

items.guidance: Components must not apply external margin. Margin between components is the responsibility of the parent layout.
items.rationale: Components that own their margins create unpredictable spacing when composed.

items.guidance: Use CSS gap as the primary mechanism for spacing between sibling elements.
items.rationale: Gap applies spacing uniformly without affecting the first or last child.

agents.intent: Document spacing scale tokens and enforce their consistent application across layout, components, and typography.

constraints.rule: Do not substitute spacing tokens with typography or icon-size tokens.
Always use the next scale step up or down rather than custom values.

disambiguation.distinction: Spacing governs whitespace and layout gaps; typography governs type size, weight, and line height.

metadata.value: How errors are surfaced through inline validation, summary banners, and toast notifications.

items.name: alert
items.role: Page-level error summary listing all current errors.

items.name: button
items.role: Submit trigger that initiates client-side validation.

items.description: Client-side validation runs on all required fields. If any fail, submission is prevented.
Each invalid field displays an inline error message. An error summary alert appears at the top of the form with anchor links to each field.
The inline error for that field is removed. When all errors are resolved, the summary disappears.

useCases.description: When a form submission fails validation and the user needs to correct one or more fields.
When the feedback is a successful outcome.

alternative.name: success-messaging
alternative.rationale: Success feedback uses success colors and icons. Reusing the error pattern for positive outcomes confuses the visual language.

items.guidance: Always display inline errors directly below the invalid field.
items.rationale: Proximity eliminates the cognitive load of mapping an error message to its field.

items.guidance: Move focus to the error summary when it appears.
items.rationale: Screen reader users will not discover the summary unless focus is moved to it.

items.guidance: Do not clear the form or reset field values when validation fails.
items.rationale: Clearing the form destroys the user's work.

agents.intent: Guide multi-component composition for surfacing, communicating, and resolving form validation errors.

constraints.rule: Always include both an inline field error and a summary when multiple fields fail.
Do not use toast notifications as the sole error communication for form validation.

disambiguation.distinction: Use error-messaging for validation and system errors; use empty-state for the absence of content.
Use error-messaging for form validation flows; use alert for non-form system or status messages.

antiPatterns.description: Showing errors only on submit without inline field feedback.
antiPatterns.instead: Show inline errors on field blur and in a summary on submit.
